CURRENT AFFAIRS,

Yet another Commission, this time a Viceregal Com-
mission of Inquiry into the Irish railways. The Free-
man makes a poor attempt to conceal its jubilation at
the latest Commission, for it, or rather its Mr. Sexton,
is one of the Commissioners. For our own part, even
though the Freeman is a poor thing, and it takes up a
lot of our energy to push it on an inch here and an
inch there, we are glad that Mr. Sexton has been placed
on the Commission. He is a good accountant, if nothing
to speak of as a statesman. The railway question was
agitated in the TLEADER five years ago, but we do not:
rocollect that the Deplorable ever showed any inibiat?ve,

or even sustained interest in the question until M.
Sexton joined in the agitation for reform in the Great
Sourface Railway. The Freeman has not shown any
great concern for the railway question, and so we are
inclined to fear that Mr. Sexton’s interest  may rank
rather as an acquired taste. However, with his un-
doubted ability for arithmetic he should prove a useful
member, if happily there should be any utility in the
Commission at all—for one finds it hard not to look
upon all these Commissions as attempts to throw dust
in tho eyes of the people.

Ireland is not only an entity, or rather should be
looked upon as an entity, by all who desire her national
development, but she is an abnormal entity. What
do Englishmen know about her? What does Sir Charles
Scotter, of an English railway, know of Ireland, or of
Ireland’s point of view? Probably being an English-
man he believes that the destiny of Ireland is to be the
fruitful mother of flocks and herds for the feeding of
John Bull, and the dumping ground fcr John Bull’s
Brummagem and shoddy. Cheap rates »ut for cattle,
and cheap rates in for shoddy may po:-"bly represent
Sir Charles Scotter’s conscientious idea of what would
be for the best interests of Ireland. Another Com-
missioner is Mr. W. M. Acworth, an English Parlia-
mentary lawyer.  Another is a Mr. Aspinall, of the
Lancs. and Yorks. Railway.

Mr. Sexton’s Freeman thinks that “The Ir ™ repre-
sentation will, we believe, give general satisfnction.”
‘We are not so sure of that; no doubt Mr. Matty ™ dkin
has already congratulated Mr. Sexton on his appoint-
ment, but even Matty Bodkin’s benediction does not
necessarily coincide with “ general satisfaction.’”” Lord
Pirrie—recently titled—we suppose is intended to repre-
sent Belfast. We know very little of Colonel Hutchin-
son Poe beyond the fact that he wrote a scurrilous and
unmanly letter with reference to those who protested
against Humphrey’s Dump, otherwise known as the
International Exhibition, at the public meeting which
was eventually outraged by the calling in of the police.
We have a doubtful idea that he is one of the “ Devolu-
tionists.”” But what has he done; what are his quali-
fications to be on this Commission? Why was not Mr.
Michael Sullivan, who has taken a great interest in
railway matters, put on instead of Colonel Hutchinson
Poe?

‘Why was Hutchinson Poe put on this Commission? If
any man should have been put on it Mr. Michael Sullivan
deserves a place, for the public would have implicit con-
fidence in his judgment, and honesty and courage. It
will be curious to observe if Mr. Sexton will come out
of this Commission better or worse than Mr. William
O’Brien emorged from the Dunraven Conference. We
hope that the sittings of the Commission will be open
to tho Press, so that tho public may have an idea of
what goes on at them. We have no unbounded con-
fidence in this Commission. We have no special reason
for saying it, but it strikes us as a Commission with
something up its sleeve.

President of the United States thought of the book, for

Father Dinncen’s Dictionary, published by the Irish
Texts Society, has met with more than remarkable suc-
cess.  Out of 5,000 copies we hear that there are now
only a few hundred left. The Education Department
in England are contemplating placing the Dictionary—
if they have not by this time done so—on their list of
requisition books in certain County Council schools. A
second edition will be required almost immediately.
This is certainly a satisfactory state of affairs in connec-
tion with a book retailing at 7s. 6d. a copy, and is an
important item of evidence of the strength of the Irish
Language Movement.

now may be accepted as a project that will shortly
materialise, has excited the descendants of those “ civic
virtue ”’ Britishers who clamoured for, and succeeded in
having pacsed, laws for the killing of competing

-industries in Ireland. = We recently referred in our
notes to a conversation that an Irish gentleman in Eng-
land had with a British boot manufacturer who was in
a state of general alarm at the quietness of Treland, and
in a state of particular alarm at the projected boot fac-
tory. Mr. Hayden, of 9 Eden Quay, who may be
described as the promoter of the projected factory, re-
ceived a very curious letter recently. The letter was type-

It is, it is true, an anonymous letter. It
is an attempt to buy over Mr. Hayden, who
is engaged in promoting the projected boot factory
in Dublin. Mr. Hayden took no notice of it. He

munication with “my syndicate’’ that wanted to wean
him away, for a consideration, from his enterprise, but
ho could not ses his way as a conscientious and honour-
able man to stoop to such methods. He therefore took
no notice of the proffered bribe.

1t will be observed that ‘ my syndicate *” are “ really
substantial people,” guaranteed to turn oub the latce?

It will further be observed that the enterprise from
which it was sought to buy Mr. Hayden is “a very
inferior undertaking,”” and that “Irish enterprises
generally fail for lack of financial support.”’ = Let us
admit it with due humility, there is a certain measure
of truth in the last taunt from the would-be buyer of
Mr. Hayden. Well, no announcement appeared in the
Forger that “ . leaves America,”’ so H. has heard
nothing further about the matter. ~ Mr. Hayden, we
may mention, in his youth fought with the Papal
Zouaves, and as an old soldier it is not for him to leave
his post.

The Hibernian Bible Society celebrated its centenary
this year, and one of its booklets, entitled “The Island of
Saints; or The Bible in Ireland till 1611,”" has fallen
into our hands. It is by the Rev. Ralph W. Harden.
We note that the Committee of this “ Hibernian ”’—
should it not be rather anti-Hibernian—Society dis-
claims responsibility for the details of the treatment of
the subjects in its booklets; it only owns up to
responsibility for the selection of its writers. The Rev.
Ralph W. Harden commences his booklet in this way :—

Two passengers were seated on a coach journcying through
the lovely scen: at delights the traveller in the south-castern
counties of Ireland. It was ecarly summer of the year of Grace
1882. Hardly a fortnight had elapsed since the world had been
scared by the appalling murders in the Phoenix Park of Mr.
Burke and Loid Frederick Cavendish. Naturally, the talk of our
trrwcllcrs turned upon the state of our country:  Ah!" exclaimed
one, “I tell you Ireland is near her deliverance. We have boen
waiting long for it. But it is come at last. Ireland’s sons arc
going to rule.  Woll banish the Saxon to his own country.
We'll drivo out the English Bible Sacioties and their Bibles for
ever; and wo’ll make Ireland onco more the Island of Saints she
was of old—

 Great, glorious and freo,
First flower of tho earth, first gom of tho soa.’ "

Needless to sS4y, 10 names are mentioned—they might
1ot bo convenient. Some parts of the pamphlet would
b> wholly amusing if they dealt with any secular sub-
ject. Wo aro told that,  St. Patrick may be said to, be
tho first to introduce the Bible Society into Treland, in
cffct_:b, a primitive Founder of every Hibernian Bible
Society of our country.’’ Again wo read— In other
words, St. Patrick was a Bible Society Colporteur and

ments that the author commits to paper concerning

ng.”” It is strange that a man who is styled “Rev.”
should write such libellous trash as that.

. We have already been lectured on our extravagance
in building churches. But even in this country, bled
al_lmosb to death, as it has been, by alien landlordism,
tithe-paying to an imported church and over-taxation,
We have never before heard “Peter’s Penco ”’ put for-

Yet; this Rev. Ralph W. Harden has tho hardihood to

It is curious a thousand years aftorwards to watch another,

where they may enjoy tho liberty of reading the Holy Book in

So the “wunceasing outflow of Irish emigrants’ is not
caused by landlordism, bad government, over-taxa-
tion, want of industry, the terrible economic drainage
of the so-called Church of Ireland; no, the ‘“ unceasing
- outflow,”” according to the Rev. Mr. Harden, is caused
by a desire to read the Bible in foreign parts, read a
book that the emigrants could buy for a few shillings
at Browne and Nolan’s, or Gill’s, and read at home.

In another part of the booklet we read :—* Brian

Boru, Ireland’s greatest monarch, a gracious and

generous promoter of a Bible Society for Ireland.”” The

" extraordinary suggestion runs through the book that

the ““Idolators’ are against the Bible! By the way,

does not tradition say that Brian Boru, Ireland’s

greatest monarch,’’ carried a crucifix at the battle of

Clontarf. Perhaps there was even a little ““idolatry ™

ay that time! Men like the Rev. Mr. Harden are

stripped of all sense of humour, if they have any to be

stripped of, when they turn their sour eyes on that

awful “Rome’’ that is such a perennial butt for

ignoramuses. Before the Danish incursions, long years

before the invention of printing, and at a time when,

according to Rev. Mr. Harden, there was not “ in manu-

script in those days, nor for many centuries after,

- " even the smallest portion of an Irish Bible,” we

are seriously informed in the preceding paragraph that—

“In short, Ireland was, in the days before the Danish

incursions, a great Bible Society House.” The Bible

in those days known to the Catholic priests in Ireland

was probably written in Latin. Iow Ireland, beforo

the Danish incursions, when the Bible was in Latin, and

only MS. copies were obtainable, became “ a great Bible

Society Fouse,” is a matter that on the face of it would
require some explaining.

Notice of this “ Hibernian Bible Socicty ”’ pamphlet
comes near the border-line of our province. ~We arc
not theologians. However, we touch on this pamphlet
as it is not a booklet on theology, but a piece of ignorant
humbug that apparently is inspired by that virulent
hatred of Catholic Ireland which finds so many channels
of expressions in those days. It is our view that it is
a mistake to let all these ignorant productions that are
swallowed as truth by hosts of dupes go unchallenged.
Bigots and retailers of libels on Catholic Ireland grow
in impudence when they find that they are not pulled up.

The fourth edition, 20th thousand, of Mr. R. J.
Kelly’s pamphlet, “On the Evils of Emigration,” has
just been published by the Catholic Truth Society of
Ireland. Tven though emigration has not heen stopped,
it is satisfactory to find that so many people have read of
tho evils of it in Mr. Kelly’s pamphlet.

In tho course of Dr. Warnock’s address at aonac an
ouin he remarked :-—‘ Now what I wish to impress here
is that every man who has got a farm of land can live
and bring up his family upon the produce of that farm,
and that those living along the seaboard have an advan-
tage over those living inland in the cheap and abundant
supply of fish to be so readily obtained.”” There
appears certainly to be great possibilities of develop-
ment in this direction. Irish landlordism with a crow-
bar in one hand and a faggot in the other, ever demand- 4
ing money, money, by way of rent, naturally over-em-
phasised money in the views of the people, and that
over-emphasis of money has to be toned away. The
farmer who sells a pig, and turns round and pays part %
of the money for a side of American bacon, is a glaring
cxample of the undue tendency to turn everything into
money, and then part with the money in exchange for
inferior stuff at inflated prices. There is great room for
reform in these directions, and the need, like other
needs, has to be dinned into the ears of those concerned,
in season and out of season, for a very long time before
it takes effect.

A prominent Irishman, in the course of a letter to us,

Mr. Samuel Young, M.P.,has run his stick into a
Souper’s nest. It came about this way:—The June
_ number of a Belfast Protestant magazine, called S%.
Thomas’ Parish Magazine, fell into his hands, and
under the heading “ Echoes from the Emerald Isle,”’ ho
read tales of the doings of two employees of the Irish
Church Missions. In a district © where the Romanists
are very numerous,”’ 200 “ Romanists ’’ and 25 Protes-
tants crowded a school-house to listen to the Irish
Church Mission lecturers. In another village “ nearly
400 Romanists *’ were present on the first two evenings.
On the third night notwithstanding “ various sports
being held that day’’ in the village, “young men and
old men, as well as women, camo trooping in.””  Tho
Irish Church Missions employee could not help saying
inwardly—so he tells the public—" Praise the Lord.’”
There were “at least 200 Romanists *’ present, and it
was soul-inspiring.””  The Irish Church Mission man
also says:—“If only English Christians could be led to
realise ‘how ignorant of the Bible and the Gospel truth
Irish Romanists really are, they would be more ready
to help a society whose main object is, etc.’”” The hat’s
the thing, you see.

Now Mr. Young was anxious for particulars of these
meetings. He first wrote to the Protestant Rector of
the Parish, the Rev. William Dowse, who is responsible
for the contents of the magazine asking for the dates
and places of these remarkable occurrences in which
“ Romanists *’ figured so largely, and in such round
numbers. Mr. Dowse replied that he had not the in-
formation required by Mr. Young, and referred Mr.
Young to the Secretary of the Irish Church Missions,
the Rev. J. R. Goff, 4 Great Brunswick Street, Dublin.

Mr. Young, nothing daunted, pursued his inquiries.
He wrote to Mr. Goff. Mr. Goff replied stating that
he had sent Mr. Young’s letter to “our Secretary in
London, who has charge of the Department, and he will
doubtless correspond with you on the subject.”” In due
course the London agent of the “ Irish Church Missions’’
wrote to Mr. Young from 11 Buckingham Street,

Strand, London:—

Dear Sir,—The Rev. J. R. Goff has sent me your letters re the
paragraphs which were inserled in St. Thomas’s Parish Magazine.
The statements contained therein were made on the authority of
our missionaries, and of the rector of the parish whero the mect-
ings were held, but as wo do not wish to subject him to the
treatment meted out to Dr. Long some time ago, I must respect-
fully and finally decline to give you the name of the locality in
which tho meetings were held, or the date on which they toolk
place. You may, however, rest assured that the statements you
refer to are true.

Mr. Young replied as follows:—

is responsible. I need not say the Rev. William Dowso is one
of the most respectable clergymen in tho diocese of Down and
Connor, He minsters to a rich, confiding peoplo, the most of
whom implicitly believe in what appears in a Church periodical,
but there are wicked sceptics, of couise, among them as elsewhero,
who .will not believe without proof or some sort of confirmation
statements which appear incredible. If you refuse to give place
and dates those who mix with and know something about the
Catholic people and the strong attachment they have for tho
religion they profess will discredit your statement. I am rather
concerned about the Rev. Mr. Dowse. What will ho say to his
people, who reasonably ask for verification? It will not be an
answer to say there is fear of persecution, because it is woll
known that persecution would help on the glorious work of your
mission in plucking brands from the burning. It is said tho
blood of the martyrs is the sced of tho Church. I fear your
refusal to give particulars will produco a bad effect in St.
L'homas’s congregationby raising doubts in tho mifnds of his innocont
_good people who now subscribo and otherwise work hard to save
the souls of these ‘Romanists.” I hope, thorofore, you will

mind in a state of quicsence. You will admit to be sent from

Wo think Mr. Young is to be thanked for having
tracked down this little story; it was a piece of Sinn
Fém work, and a class of work that we think is very
much noglected. After all,” however absurd and gro-
tesque these stories appear to those who know “Idola-
trous *’ Ireland, the fact remains that there are tens of
of thousands of simple people who take them as un-
diluted truth, and it were time that the “Idolators’” -
stirred themselves. For our own part, the tales on the
faco of them strike us as ludicrous, and in view of the
refusal to attempt to substantiate them when challenged
wo have no hesitation in describing them as fabrications.
The Soupers had better be more careful in future.
“Tchoes from the Emerald Isle’’ is a very catching
heading, but unfortunately for the Soupers, we are not
altogether so emerald in this country as we used to be.

The Lynz is the name of the first number of a new
sixpenny magazine conducted by the students of the
Royal College of Science for Ireland. It is intended to
bring it out threo times during the College Session.
Like all college magazines, it, of course, only appeals to
a limited section of the public. It appears_to be an
oxcellent specimen of its class, and our readers will be
pleased to hear that it contains two articles in Irish.
The Irish-Ireland idea is kept well to the front, and the
School of Art Gaelic Society, which was established
under the presidency of the late Mr. R. N. A. Willis,
whose untimely death was a public loss, appears to be
doing excellent work. There is one “poem ’’ in Eng-
lish called “ Eliza J.”” that scarcely deserves a place in
The Lynz ; but perhaps the conductors consider that the
tastes of the “ Hell of a fellow ’’ have to be catered for
as well as those of the scientist and the Irishman.

“Clap " writes us:—“ When opening the Clare Feis
last Saturday, the Bishop of Killaloe referred to the
very strenuous and effectual part the Lraper has taken
in tho Irish Revival. ~He said that the Irish-
Ireland movement, backed wup by the magni-

HE Language Movement is about thirtecn years

of age. That is not a very advanced age. In

fact, it is only passing from childhood into boyhood.

And still, the whole country is swinging and oscillating

and vibrating under the weight and vigour of its
striding footsteps as it marches along.

Thirteen years ago its footsteps were the toddlings
of a baby learning to walk. Thoughtful persons, i.e.,
many who considered themselves thoughtful persons,
had no idea that the toddler would live to stand and
walk without help. I well remember the toddler’s
attempts at walking, in the city of Cork, thirteen years
ago. The attempts were feeble. Wise people looked
and shook their heads, as much as to say, ‘“ What mad-
ness! ”’  But the toddler refused to die. He learned
to stand alone. Then he learned to walk. He grew
steadily. TIis growth was slow during the first few
years. Ie was unnoticed except by the few who were
interested in him and who were endeavouring to nurse
him and to keep him alive in spite of a great many
adverse circumstances. As time passed he began to be
taken notice of. Only a few of the city people took
any notice of him. Some of the country people, all
over Munster, began to take a great deal of notice of
him and to be interested in him long before the I«
the city people seemed to be at all aware of his
ence. Once he advanced out of the toddling stage he
began to grow rapidly. With his growth his strength
began to increase. ~ With his strength his marching
power increased. Soon the sound of his footsteps was
heard all over Munster. That sound was echoing and
reverberating all over Munster for years before any of
the other provinces was even stirring in its sleep. At
iast the commotion in Munster roused up the three pro-
vinces. Now our Boy of thirteen is marching and
striding and stamping through every inch of the four
provinces and the whole country is swinging and oscil-
lating and vibrating under the weight of his powerful
footsteps. Those who saw him toddling, unsteadily as
they thought, thirteen years, what are they doing now ?
Wonderful to relate, they are all taking him as a matter
of course! They are all falling into line, shoulder to
shoulder, and adapting their steps to his marchings!
Such is the fascinating influence of innate power.
They never thought there was such power in him, and
thoy are asking themselves the question: ““ If his energy
is so surprising at the age of thirteen, what will it be
at thirty ¥’

T should he inclined to answer them in this way: < It
is lawful to judge the future by the past. Thirteen
years ago you had not even a remote idea of the nature
of the thing which you see now before your eyes, and
which is called the Irish Language Movement. After
tho lapse of time shall have turned the thirteen
years into thirty years you will, if you live so long, see
a thing of which you have just as remote an idea now
as you had thirteen years ago of what you see now.””

THE IRISH COMIC PICTURE POSTCARD.

N recent years we have heard a good deal about the
war which the Irish in America are waging upon
the stage Irishman. The new attitude of the American
Irish on that subject shows a healthy change from
former times. Can we say we have had a correspond-
ing change at home? True, the stage Irishman is not
53 much seen upon the stage now as formerly ; indeed,
he is hardly seen at all, and then only in a very tame
form. But what may De called the stage Irishman has
long existed elsewhere than on the stage. ITe existed
in idiotic jokes in the pseudo-comic papers, where he
.mouthed very fearfully, and said ‘‘ Phwat ’’ for What,
and ““Sorr* for Sir. e existed, too, in a pictorial
form in these same comics (whenever they were
illustrated), and had impossible knee-breeches, impro-
bable shoes, and an unlikely hat—the latter with an
irrelevant pipe always stuck in the front of it. As
these things—jokes and pictures—appeared in Saxon
publications, we had no way of controlling them. We
could refrain from buying them, a course which some
took, and some did not; more shame on them. But
in addition to these things there were the hog-oak
“ ornaments,’”’ with the inevitable “ Paddy aund his
pig.”’ There were, too, the photo cards, of which
Laurence’s and the now extinct house of Reilly and
‘Co., in Grafton Street, always kept a supply. Paren-
thetically, T understand that Reilly was a Methodist.
I remember once looking at Reilly’s window, when a
couple of young swells came to look at a photo of a
man named Cook, a particularly undistinguished-
looking individual. The young fellows thought it was
Ceok, the famous billiard-player, and were amusingly
disgusted at finding that Cook was a dissenting
preacher, doubtless of the same persuasion as Reilly
himself.  Reilly’s’’ did a large trade in the comic
Irish photos, and so did other houses in the same street,
too. But to-day we have entered on a new phase, and
‘the ““ comic’’ photo picture is now issued as a picture
postcard—coloured ! -Moreover, it is everywhere. 1
saw some of them in a shop window on the Quays; in
the other parts of the window were religious prints,
prayer-books, rosaries, crucifixes, and so on. In another
shop, this time in Parliament Street, I saw the same
thing—‘‘ comic ’’ picture postcards of impossible Irish-
men, side by side with Catholic prayer-books, rosaries,
and other religious wares.

This popularisation of the comic photo of former
days gives me to think. The old photo picture cost
more than the picture postcard, and so was less widely
disseminated for sale. Now, these ‘‘ comic’’ nuisances
-can be had cheap, and coloured, to boot, and their sale
is likely to be much more widespread.  Well, the
thoughts which these things set going in my mind are
many-sided. Is the Language movement doing any
good? Is the Gaelic League any use? Have our
people any sense of decency? Or shame? Above all,
have they any sense of humour? There is no humour
in these pictures. =~ Whom, then, do they amuse? T
had really thought we had got beyond this sort of
‘thing ; that the LEapER, and the Language movement,
and modern developments in general had wiped out our
people’s hankering after this sort of stulf. Moreover,
I had supposed that this kind of trade was confined

to the fashionable fancy goods houses, which sold
‘“souvenirs ’’ to tourists. But it secms that was a
delusion, for we now find these silly things sold in the
:same shops as sell Catholic prayer-books, rosaries and
religious prints, and these, too, in neighbourhoods not:
much frequented by the tourist. It all tends to set
‘deep reflections going.

As a people I fear we have not much self-respect;
not much dignity; worst of all, perhaps, not much
‘notion of humour. Fancy an Englishman laughing at
a picture of a typical John Bull pouring hot water
down a hen’s neck ‘‘to make her lay biled eggs’! T
can hardly fancy it.  Punch has often ridiculed the
Britisher, both by word and picture, or rather, it has
often ridiculed actual faults, and possible or probable
§¥1ortcomings of the British character. But it prints

othing from which we could only infer that John Bull

THE SECRET OF THE CELL.*

F you bring certain substances together, say a half-
crown and some quick-silver, they will, as the

chamist shows us, shortly combine before our eyes
to form a new substance, an amalgamation of the two.
So other substances will produce flame when brought
together ; others will explode. All this is the matter of
chemistry. Again we all know the effects of )7u)1e_5ys,
levers, falling bodies and flowing liquids, and the like.
They are governed by the laws of Natural Philosophy.
Is there, then, anything else going on in the body of a
man or animal, save these effects of Chemistry and
Natural Philosophy? Is a dog or the bodily part of a
man aught else save a system of levers, ropes and water-
courses which, by bringing substances such as milk and
ga juice together, enable them to produce chemical
combinations that create blood and flesh? Or is there
something more in even the lowest form of living
thing, be it plant or vegetable, that makes it differ
from all things whatsoever that are without life? Is
there something more even in a jelly-fish than can be
produced by any chance mingling of lifeless things, or
any seb of amalgamations like that of the half-crown
and the quick-silver? This is the subject to which Dr.
Windle devotes himself in his little brochure—*The
Secret of the Cell.” “ What is life?’ is really the
problem he sets to himself. What is the gap, if, as our
Catholic philosophy teaches, there is a gap, that sepa-
rates the lowest form of life from non-life? Is there a
chasm that can never be bridged between the rock and
tho limpet, between the plant and the soil, and what
is it? That there should be any doubt on the question
may, to ordinary men, seem strange, but not a few
distinguished writers have maintained that there is
really nothing more in the self-support of a living crea-
ture than in the flowing of water or the assimilation of
the quick-silver by the half-crown. Now every living
being consists of one or more cells. Some low forms of
animal consist of only one. But in animals such as
man there are innumerable cells. Yet each one of these
cells possesses wonderful powers. It supports itself; it
can repair itself, and it develops in accordanca with a
pre-ordained type. And Dr. Windle’s thesis—and I
think he proves his case—is that in these wonderful
powers of sustenance, repair and development that con-
stituts life there is a force of which chemistry and
Natural Philosophy can tell us nothing, the great mys-
tery, tho force of life. The power of providing fqr
their fubure existence that all living things have, is
something that no thing without li.fe can, by any acci- 2
dent, develop. The chasm is impossible. The little book
is so interesting that I read it through in a sitting; and
T can recommend it as a contribution to science from a
Catholic point of view that seems to bring an abstruse
subject entirely within the comprehension of an ordi-
nary reader. Personally I know nothing of Biology. .
As was said of Pascal with Theology, so it may be said i
of Dr. Windle that on this occasion, at any rate, he has
brought Biology into our parlours. C.

We called a young gentleman in the drapery business
into consultation, and he at once gave it as his opinion

not believe that nuns’ veiling is made in Ireland.

“But it was sent for Irish, for she only asked for

Irish they would have written ‘Irish’ on it, like the
other patterns, and they have not done so.” No, they
had not done so. But apparently they had no objec-
tion to tacitly practising a bit of fraud on a customer

writing! My friend returned the patterns, and sent
to ’s, and got overy bit as good a quality as the
Is. 64d. for 1s. per yard, about the same width, too.
Thanks to the Leaper, his readers aro prefty wide
awako, and not a bit inclined to pay 64d. more per yard
for the privilege of being cheated.

Now everyone will admit that this sort of thing is
extremely discouraging, especially to working people
who have not much time for their shopping. As far as
working girls are concerned, the usual timo for leaving
off work is 6.30 p.m., and most of the large shops close
at 6 o’clock. So this only leaves them Saturday after-
noon, and this is such a busy time for the shops one
could hardly expect to have much heed paid to a demand
for “Irish.” However, some workers could go shop-
ping at their dinner hour once in a while.

Another matter I must refer to, at the risk of being
wearisome, and that is, that speaking generally work-"
ing girls cannot afford from 2s. 6d. to bs. 6d. per yard
for their dresses, yet the only alternative is English
manufacture. But Belfast is not made up of working
givls! No, there are also working men and their wives
who can still less afford big prices, because, unfortu-
nately, a man’s earnings do not increase with each addi-
tion to his family, yet clothing, etc., has to be provided
for all. I am supposing the case of a family where the
man brings home all his earnings; unfortunately this is
not generally the rule in Belfast, for between drink and
betting, the wife and mother has often to work herself
to enable the family to exist. Now I ask, is it fair to
such women for Irish manufacturers to penalise them
by providing nothing cheap, and thus obliging them to
buy foreign goods?

If “shoddy ’’ be a necessity—and it is such for hun-
dreds of people—let it be Irish-made shoddy. Really
when one takes time to consider it, there is a wonderful
variety of things needed every day which do not seem
to be made in Ireland at all. What about machine-
mado lace? What about coloured silks for blouses, and
millinery, which we can obtain in Belfast shops from
114d. per yard upwards? Are these things not made
in Treland? And why not? Of course, wo can buy
Irish poplin! Oh, yes! at 4s. 11d. per yard!

I wish people would cultivate a little common sense.
Some energetic young fellows have boen addressing Sinn
Fém meetings, advising people to buy Irish mauuf'ac-
turo “regardless of the economic cant about its being
as good and as cheap.”’ Apparently they think that
Trish manufacturers and traders are all saints and
angels here below. Is it fair to har:.l—wolrking p?ople
to ask them to pay any price for a thing if only it be
Trish? Even Irish manufacturers are only human,
and it would be no cause for wonder, if they were in-
clined to take advantage when they find they can get
as much as they like to ask for Irish manufacture. I
do not wish to disparage our young lecturors; they are
doing good work, and may do still better work if they
will adopt the “as-good-and-as-cheap "’ principle when
addressing the working people. Those who have more
money can, of course, pay as much as they choose.‘ But
